task_prompt: A team reports their new recommendation model achieves 72% accuracy, beating the reported baseline of 68%. The baseline was evaluated on the full test set; the new model was evaluated on a 10% random sample of the same test set. Conclude whether the improvement is valid.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Result is invalid: unequal evaluation conditions render the comparison meaningless without confidence intervals confirming the gap exceeds sampling variance.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Re-evaluate both models on the identical full test set and report accuracy with 95% confidence intervals.

planted_issues.description: New model was evaluated on 10% sample; baseline on full set. A 4-point gap on a 10% sample has wide confidence intervals that may include zero.
No statistical significance test or confidence interval reported for the accuracy difference.

ideal_critique: The 10% sample evaluation for the new model introduces substantial sampling variance. On a typical test set of 10,000 examples, a 10% sample (1,000 examples) yields a margin of error of roughly ±3 points at 95% confidence — making the 4-point gap statistically indistinguishable from zero.
Evaluation conditions must be identical to enable valid comparison. The baseline was measured on the full set; re-measuring on the same full set is required before any claim of improvement.
ideal_defense: The defense should concede that the sampling discrepancy is a fatal confound and commit to re-running evaluation on the full test set.
The defense may legitimately note that directional evidence is suggestive, but must not claim the improvement is established.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Full-set evaluation shows the gap shrinks below 1 point or reverses.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Full-set evaluation shows the gap remains above 3 points and is statistically significant at p < 0.05.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Full-set gap is 1–3 points without a clear significance threshold agreed in advance.

notes: Trivial-looking flaw that should be caught immediately. Tests whether critique recognizes evaluation asymmetry as fatal.

task_prompt: An NLP team compares a fine-tuned BERT model against a 'majority-class baseline' on a sentiment classification task. The majority-class baseline always predicts 'positive'. The dataset is 60% positive, 20% negative, 20% neutral. The BERT model achieves 78% accuracy vs. the baseline's 60%. The team claims BERT is 18 points better. Is this a valid baseline comparison?

ground_truth.final_verdict: The accuracy comparison is technically correct but the majority-class baseline is inappropriate for a 3-class imbalanced problem. Macro-F1 or per-class recall should be the primary metric. BERT likely still wins, but the margin and the nature of the improvement are misrepresented.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Report macro-F1, per-class precision/recall/F1, and confusion matrices for both the majority-class baseline and BERT.

planted_issues.description: Majority-class baseline is a degenerate classifier that ignores negative and neutral classes entirely. It is a floor, not a meaningful comparison point.
78% accuracy on a 60/20/20 split could be achieved by a model that correctly handles positives but fails on negatives and neutrals. Macro-F1 would reveal this.

ideal_critique: The majority-class baseline is degenerate: it scores 0% recall on negative and neutral classes. Beating it proves only that BERT is better than doing nothing — not that BERT is useful.
Accuracy is the wrong metric for an imbalanced 3-class problem. Macro-F1 is the appropriate primary metric and may show a much smaller advantage or expose class-level failures in the BERT model.
ideal_defense: The defense should concede that macro-F1 and per-class metrics are necessary and that the claimed 18-point improvement is an overstatement of practical significance.
The defense may legitimately note that BERT almost certainly beats random and majority baselines, but the strength of that claim requires proper metrics.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: BERT macro-F1 is below 0.70 or recall on negative/neutral classes is below 0.50.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: BERT macro-F1 exceeds 0.75 and all per-class recalls exceed 0.65, confirming genuine multi-class competence.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: BERT macro-F1 is 0.70–0.75 with uneven per-class performance.

notes: Case where defense partially wins: BERT probably is better, but the framing is wrong. Tests mixed-verdict reasoning.

task_prompt: A team proposes a new tabular ML model for predicting customer churn. They compare against a logistic regression baseline trained on the same features. Their model achieves AUC-ROC 0.88 vs. baseline 0.81. However, the new model uses 47 features; the logistic regression baseline uses only 12 features (a legacy subset). The team claims the new model is 'architecturally superior'. Evaluate the claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The architectural superiority claim is not supported. The feature set difference is a confound: the new model may win purely due to additional features. A fair comparison requires logistic regression trained on all 47 features.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Train logistic regression (with regularization sweep) on all 47 features and compare AUC-ROC against the new model on the same train/test split.

planted_issues.description: New model uses 47 features; baseline uses 12. The AUC gap may be entirely attributable to additional features, not architecture.
Claiming 'architectural superiority' when feature set is not controlled is an attribution error — performance gain could be from data, not inductive bias.
No ablation isolating architecture vs. feature contribution is reported.

ideal_critique: The comparison is confounded: 47 features vs. 12 features makes it impossible to attribute the AUC gap to architecture vs. data richness.

The claim of 'architectural superiority' requires controlling for features. A logistic regression with all 47 features is frequently competitive with gradient-boosted models on tabular data and must be tested.
An ablation — new model trained on only the 12 baseline features — would further isolate the architectural contribution.
ideal_defense: The defense should concede that the feature-set confound is real and that the architectural superiority claim requires an apples-to-apples feature comparison.
The defense may note that the combined system (new architecture + new features) is practically useful, but the framing of the claim must change.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Logistic regression on all 47 features achieves AUC within 0.02 of the new model.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Logistic regression on all 47 features achieves AUC below 0.84, and the new model achieves 0.88, with gap statistically significant.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Logistic regression on 47 features achieves AUC 0.84–0.87.

notes: Tests whether the agent can identify a feature-set confound distinct from metric mismatch. Hard because the raw numbers look convincing.

task_prompt: A fraud detection model is evaluated and found to achieve 99.5% accuracy on a dataset where 0.4% of transactions are fraudulent. The team reports this as a success. Evaluate the metric choice.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Accuracy is uninformative for extreme class imbalance. A model predicting 'not fraud' for every transaction achieves 99.6% accuracy. Precision, recall, and F1 on the fraud class — or AUC-PR — are the required metrics.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Compute and report precision, recall, F1 on the positive (fraud) class, and area under the precision-recall curve (AUC-PR).

planted_issues.description: With 0.4% fraud prevalence, a trivial always-negative classifier achieves 99.6% accuracy, beating the reported 99.5%. The metric does not distinguish the model from a degenerate classifier.

ideal_critique: The trivial always-negative baseline scores 99.6% accuracy, which is higher than the reported 99.5%. This means the reported metric cannot confirm the model is doing anything useful at all.
Fraud detection requires high recall on the fraud class. Accuracy on a 0.4% prevalence dataset is the canonical example of a misleading metric and should never be the primary evaluation.
ideal_defense: The defense must concede that accuracy is the wrong metric here and that the reported number is not evidence of model usefulness.
The defense may note that operational precision/recall thresholds depend on business cost ratios, but cannot defend accuracy as the primary metric.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Fraud-class recall is below 0.50 or the model's AUC-PR is below 0.50.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Fraud-class recall exceeds 0.85 and precision exceeds 0.70, meaning the model is genuinely good despite the misleading reporting.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Fraud-class recall is 0.50–0.85 with varying precision.

notes: Canonical easy case. Tests baseline competence at recognizing imbalanced-class accuracy failure. Trivial baseline (always-negative) is competitive by the reported metric.

task_prompt: A ranking model for a search engine is evaluated using Mean Average Precision at 10 (MAP@10). The team reports MAP@10 improved from 0.41 to 0.47. The product team cares about user engagement: specifically, whether users find a satisfactory result and click it within the top 3 results. Evaluate whether MAP@10 is the right metric for this product goal.

ground_truth.final_verdict: MAP@10 is a reasonable offline proxy but does not directly measure the product goal. NDCG@3 or MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) are more aligned with 'satisfactory result in top 3'. The MAP@10 gain may or may not translate to the product metric; online A/B testing is required.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run an A/B test measuring click-through rate on position 1–3 results and session success rate (user found answer without reformulating query).

planted_issues.description: MAP@10 averages precision over positions 1–10 and weights all relevant documents equally. The product goal is top-3 satisfaction, which is better captured by MRR or NDCG@3.
Offline MAP@10 gains do not guarantee online engagement improvements due to position bias, user behavior shifts, and query distribution differences.

ideal_critique: MAP@10 weighs improvements at position 8 equally with position 1. If the product goal is top-3 satisfaction, a model could improve MAP@10 by improving positions 4–10 while degrading positions 1–3.
Offline evaluation metrics systematically miss user behavior effects. A 0.06 MAP@10 improvement does not imply an engagement lift; an A/B test is required.
ideal_defense: The defense can legitimately note that MAP@10 is a widely accepted offline proxy and the 0.06 improvement is meaningful signal worth pursuing to A/B test.
The defense should concede that NDCG@3 or MRR would be more aligned with the stated product goal and that offline results are insufficient to claim a product win.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A/B test shows no significant lift in top-3 CTR or session success rate despite MAP@10 gain.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A/B test shows significant lift in top-3 CTR and session success rate, validating MAP@10 as a proxy.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A/B test shows mixed signals: top-3 CTR improves but session success rate is flat.

notes: Case where defense partially wins: MAP@10 is not wrong, just imperfectly aligned. Tests nuanced metric critique vs. overclaiming.

task_prompt: A generative summarization model is evaluated with ROUGE-L score. The new model achieves ROUGE-L 0.42 vs. the baseline's 0.38. A human evaluation study (n=50 annotators) rated the new model's summaries as 'preferred' 55% of the time vs. 45% for baseline. The team claims both metrics agree. Evaluate the strength of this evidence.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The human evaluation is underpowered and the preference margin (55% vs 45%) is not statistically significant at n=50. ROUGE-L alignment with human preference at this sample size is not confirmatory. A properly powered human study (n~400) or a calibrated automatic metric (BERTScore, G-Eval) is required.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Run a powered human preference study (minimum n=200 independent pairwise comparisons) with inter-annotator agreement reported, or use BERTScore/G-Eval as a calibrated automatic metric alongside ROUGE-L.

planted_issues.description: n=50 annotators for a 55%/45% split has low statistical power. A two-proportion z-test at this margin and sample size yields p > 0.15 — not significant.
The team claims ROUGE-L and human evaluation 'agree', but the human eval result is not significant. Agreement with noise is not agreement.
No inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa or Krippendorff's alpha) is reported for the human study.

ideal_critique: A 55/45 human preference split at n=50 is not statistically significant (p ≈ 0.16 by binomial test). Claiming the two metrics 'agree' on the basis of non-significant human eval is misleading.
ROUGE-L is known to correlate weakly with human judgment for abstractive summarization. The underpowered human study cannot validate or invalidate that correlation.
No inter-annotator agreement is reported; without it, the 55/45 split could reflect annotator confusion rather than genuine preference.
ideal_defense: The defense can legitimately note that ROUGE-L improvement is real and directionally consistent with the (non-significant) human preference.
The defense should concede that the human study is underpowered and that 'agreement' is overclaimed. The correct claim is 'directional consistency pending a powered study'.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Powered human study (n=200+) shows no significant preference or reversal.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Powered human study (n=200+) shows preference > 58% significant at p < 0.05.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Powered study shows 53–58% preference with p between 0.05 and 0.20.

notes: False-positive trap: ROUGE gain is real, human eval directionally consistent — tempting to call this a win. The flaw is statistical underpowering, not metric invalidity.

task_prompt: A team trains a medical image classifier to detect pneumonia from chest X-rays. Training data comes from Hospital A (2018–2020). Test data comes from Hospital A (2021). The model achieves AUC 0.94. The team deploys to Hospital B and observes AUC 0.71. They attribute the drop to 'different patient demographics'. Evaluate whether this attribution is well-supported.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The demographic attribution is speculative. The performance drop is more likely explained by scanner model differences, image preprocessing pipelines, or radiograph acquisition protocols between hospitals — known sources of domain shift in medical imaging that should be investigated first.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Audit scanner model, acquisition protocol, and preprocessing pipeline for both hospitals. Run ablation with intensity normalization and histogram equalization applied to Hospital B images. Report AUC after normalization.

planted_issues.description: Team attributes AUC drop to demographics without ruling out scanner/acquisition confounds, which are the dominant source of domain shift in radiology AI literature.
No systematic audit of image acquisition parameters, scanner manufacturer, or preprocessing differences between Hospital A and B is reported.
Train/test split mixes temporal shift (2018–2020 vs 2021) with site shift. The 2021 data from Hospital A may itself have distribution shift, making the 0.94 AUC an optimistic estimate.

ideal_critique: In medical imaging, scanner hardware and acquisition protocol differences are the dominant causes of cross-site performance degradation, not patient demographics. The demographic attribution is the most speculative explanation and should be last to investigate, not first.

The team's test set is from the same hospital as training, creating a site-homogeneous evaluation. The 0.94 AUC may be inflated by site-specific image artifacts that the model learned as spurious features.
A proper domain shift audit requires matching scanner models, kVp settings, and preprocessing steps before attributing variance to clinical factors.
ideal_defense: The defense may note that demographic differences are a legitimate hypothesis in medical AI and cannot be entirely dismissed.
The defense should concede that scanner/protocol audit is a prior and more testable hypothesis, and that the current attribution lacks supporting evidence.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: After image normalization to Hospital B scanner characteristics, AUC recovers to above 0.85.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Image normalization yields AUC below 0.80 and demographic stratification reveals significant subgroup gaps.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Normalization yields AUC 0.80–0.85 and demographic stratification shows mixed results.

notes: Tests whether agent avoids the false-positive trap of assuming demographic bias before technical confounds are ruled out.

task_prompt: An e-commerce team runs an A/B test of a new product recommendation algorithm. Treatment group (new algorithm) shows 8% lift in add-to-cart rate over 14 days (p=0.002). However, the test ran during a promotional sale event in days 8–14 that was not pre-planned. The team claims the result is valid because randomization was maintained throughout. Evaluate this claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The post-hoc promotional event is a treatment-interaction confound. Even with valid randomization, if the new algorithm's advantage is amplified or reduced by sale conditions, the measured 8% lift reflects a mixture of algorithm effect and sale-interaction effect that cannot be separated without further analysis.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Segment the A/B result into pre-sale (days 1–7) and sale period (days 8–14) and test for a significant interaction effect between treatment assignment and sale period.

planted_issues.description: An unplanned promotional event during the latter half of the test creates a potential interaction between treatment condition and sale status. Randomization ensures balance but does not prevent interaction effects from inflating the estimate.
New recommendation algorithm may drive higher engagement during a sale due to novelty-driven exploration, inflating the lift estimate for steady-state conditions.
A low p-value confirms the measured difference is unlikely under the null hypothesis, but does not confirm the measured difference is free of confounding.

ideal_critique: Randomization ensures internal validity under the assumption that the treatment effect is constant across the test period. If the promotional event interacts with the algorithm (e.g., the new algorithm surfaces sale items better), the 8% lift is an upward-biased estimate of steady-state performance.
The p=0.002 result confirms statistical significance of the measured effect, not causal cleanness. A pre/post-sale segment analysis is required to detect interaction effects.
Standard A/B testing best practice requires stopping or flagging tests when unplanned external events occur during the measurement window.
ideal_defense: The defense can legitimately note that randomization was maintained and both groups were equally exposed to the sale, so the measured lift is real within the observed conditions.
The defense should concede that external validity (generalization to non-sale periods) is threatened and that segmented analysis is needed before deploying as a permanent change.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Pre-sale segment (days 1–7) shows lift below 3% or non-significant; sale period drives the majority of the effect.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Pre-sale segment shows lift of 6%+ and sale period shows similar or smaller incremental lift, indicating consistent algorithm advantage.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Pre-sale lift is 4–6% with p between 0.05 and 0.15 — directional but inconclusive.

notes: False-positive trap: randomization is intact, p-value is significant — tempting to call the result valid. The flaw is external validity and interaction confound, not internal validity.

task_prompt: A language model is fine-tuned for code completion on a dataset of 500k Python functions scraped from GitHub (2015–2022). It is evaluated on a held-out test set drawn from the same GitHub corpus. The model achieves pass@1 of 0.68 on the test set. The team then evaluates on a new benchmark of hand-written coding problems and observes pass@1 of 0.31. They attribute the drop to 'benchmark difficulty'. Evaluate this attribution.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The primary explanation for the gap is data contamination risk and distribution shift, not benchmark difficulty. The test set drawn from the same GitHub corpus likely overlaps with training data or shares stylistic patterns, inflating pass@1 to 0.68. The hand-written benchmark is the uncontaminated measure.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Perform n-gram overlap analysis between the training corpus and both test sets. Report the fraction of test problems with >50% 8-gram overlap with any training example.

planted_issues.description: Test set drawn from the same GitHub corpus as training creates high risk of overlap. Deduplication by function name is insufficient; semantic near-duplicates from forks and copies are common.
Claiming the hand-written benchmark is 'harder' without measuring problem difficulty (e.g., cyclomatic complexity, human solve rates) is unsupported.
In-distribution test set pass@1 of 0.68 may reflect memorization of training patterns rather than generalizable code generation skill.

ideal_critique: The GitHub test set likely contains near-duplicate or stylistically similar code to the training set. In code models, corpus overlap consistently inflates in-distribution benchmark scores above true generalization performance.
The claim that the hand-written benchmark is 'harder' is unverified. A more parsimonious explanation is that 0.31 is the true generalization performance and 0.68 is inflated by data contamination.
A proper evaluation requires deduplication by semantic similarity (not just exact match) and n-gram overlap analysis between training and test data.
ideal_defense: The defense may note that hand-written benchmarks introduce their own biases (problem selection, problem style) and that difficulty is a plausible partial explanation.
The defense should concede that contamination analysis is mandatory and that the in-distribution 0.68 pass@1 cannot be cited as evidence of generalization until overlap is quantified.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: More than 20% of in-distribution test problems show >50% 8-gram overlap with training data.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Overlap analysis shows less than 5% contamination and difficulty analysis confirms hand-written benchmark has higher cyclomatic complexity.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Overlap is 5–20% and difficulty analysis is mixed.

notes: Tests contamination reasoning for code models. The difficulty-attribution is a tempting but unverified alternative explanation.

task_prompt: A product team asks a data scientist to 'improve model performance'. The data scientist retrains the model with 3x more data and reports a 5-point accuracy improvement. The product team is disappointed — they wanted the model to run faster at inference time, not be more accurate. Evaluate whether the data scientist's work addressed the stated goal.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The data scientist solved the wrong problem. 'Improve model performance' was interpreted as predictive accuracy; the product team meant latency/throughput performance. Both parties share responsibility for the ambiguity, but the solution is technically valid for a different goal.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Measure and report inference latency (p50, p99) and throughput (queries per second) for both the original and retrained model on target hardware.

planted_issues.description: The phrase 'improve model performance' was not disambiguated between predictive performance and runtime performance before work began.

The data scientist optimized for training throughput and data ingestion speed (more data → better accuracy) rather than inference latency. These are governed by entirely different architectural choices: training throughput depends on batch size and data pipeline speed, while inference latency depends on model depth, quantization, and serving infrastructure. Adding more data does not affect inference latency; only model architecture and parameter count do.

ideal_critique: The data scientist failed to clarify the optimization target before beginning work. 'Performance' in an ML context is ambiguous and should always be disambiguated at project kickoff.

The data scientist optimized for the wrong target: training throughput and predictive accuracy rather than inference latency. Data volume does not affect inference latency; the levers for inference latency are model architecture, parameter count, quantization, and serving infrastructure — none of which were addressed.
ideal_defense: The defense may note that accuracy improvement is a legitimate interpretation of 'model performance' and that the ambiguity is shared between both parties.
The defense should concede that the product need was not clarified and that inference profiling should have been performed before and after to assess both dimensions.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Retrained model has equal or higher inference latency than the original model.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Retrained model has lower inference latency due to incidental improvements (e.g., model was retrained with quantization), making it both more accurate and faster.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Latency is unchanged and accuracy improved — work is neutral on the product goal.

notes: Easy scope-intent case. Tests whether agent can distinguish predictive performance from runtime performance without overclaiming.

task_prompt: A team is asked to 'reduce churn' using ML. They build a churn prediction model achieving AUC 0.85 and identify the top 500 at-risk users per week. They report the project as complete. The business stakeholder expected an intervention system — not just a prediction system. Evaluate whether the project met its intent.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The prediction component is a necessary but insufficient part of the full scope. 'Reduce churn' implies a causal intervention pipeline; prediction alone cannot reduce churn unless paired with an action (discount, outreach, product fix). The team delivered a valid first step but overclaimed completion.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Design and run an A/B test: identify at-risk users via the model, randomly assign to treatment (intervention) and control (no action), and measure 30-day churn rate difference.

planted_issues.description: Predicting churn does not reduce churn. Reduction requires a downstream action whose causal effect is measured. The team conflated building a prediction model with solving the business problem.
No intervention (e.g., coupon, support outreach, in-app message) was designed or tested. Without an intervention, the prediction model has no mechanism to affect the outcome.

ideal_critique: Churn prediction enables churn reduction only when coupled with an intervention. Delivering a prediction model without an intervention design and causal test does not address the stated business goal.
The team should have clarified at project start whether the deliverable was a prediction model, an intervention system, or both. Reporting the project as 'complete' misrepresents the scope gap.
ideal_defense: The defense can legitimately claim that building a high-quality prediction model (AUC 0.85) is a significant and valuable deliverable that creates the foundation for intervention.
The defense should concede that 'reduce churn' as a goal requires a closed-loop intervention and measurement system, and that the project as delivered addresses only the first step.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A/B test of intervention on model-identified at-risk users shows no significant reduction in 30-day churn rate.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: A/B test shows significant reduction in 30-day churn (p < 0.05) confirming the prediction model enables effective intervention.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: A/B test shows directional reduction but is underpowered (p between 0.05 and 0.20).

notes: Defense partially wins: the prediction work is genuinely valuable. Tests whether agent can hold 'good work, wrong scope' simultaneously.

task_prompt: A research team is asked to 'evaluate whether LLM-generated code is safe to deploy in production'. They run a static analysis tool (Bandit) on 1,000 LLM-generated Python snippets and report that 94% pass with no high-severity warnings. They conclude LLM-generated code is safe for production use. Evaluate this conclusion.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The conclusion dramatically overreaches the evidence. Static analysis for known vulnerability patterns is one necessary but far-from-sufficient dimension of production safety. The evaluation ignores logical correctness, runtime behavior, edge case handling, dependency risks, semantic security issues, and production-specific requirements. The scope of 'safe for production' far exceeds what Bandit measures.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: Define a multi-dimensional safety rubric covering: (1) logical correctness against test suites, (2) behavioral fuzzing for edge cases, (3) semantic security review (business logic flaws not detectable by static analysis), (4) dependency audit, and (5) domain-expert review of a random sample. Report pass rates per dimension.

planted_issues.description: Production safety encompasses correctness, reliability, maintainability, and security. Static analysis covers only a subset of known security anti-patterns and does not address correctness or logic flaws.
Bandit detects syntactic security anti-patterns (e.g., use of eval(), hardcoded passwords). It does not detect logical vulnerabilities, race conditions, resource leaks, or semantic security flaws.

The 1,000 snippets are not described in terms of complexity, domain, or type. Simple snippets trivially pass static analysis; production code often involves complex multi-file interactions not tested in isolation.
Even if the static analysis result were meaningful, concluding that all LLM-generated code is 'safe for production use' from 1,000 snippets is an unwarranted generalization.

ideal_critique: Static analysis with Bandit is a narrow instrument. Passing Bandit does not establish correctness, logical safety, or absence of semantic vulnerabilities — all of which are central to production safety.

The conclusion 'LLM-generated code is safe for production use' is an overgeneralization. Even if the 1,000 snippets passed, a production deployment decision requires testing across the specific domain, complexity level, and integration context of actual use.
The scope of the research question ('safe to deploy in production') requires a multi-dimensional evaluation framework, not a single static analysis pass rate.
ideal_defense: The defense may note that the Bandit pass rate is one valid data point suggesting LLM-generated code does not systematically introduce known vulnerability patterns.

The defense should concede that the conclusion is overstated and that the study addresses only a narrow slice of production safety. A scoped conclusion ('94% of tested snippets pass Bandit high-severity checks') would be defensible.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: A multi-dimensional safety evaluation reveals correctness failures, semantic vulnerabilities, or domain-specific failures in the same snippet set.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Multi-dimensional evaluation including test-suite pass rates, expert review, and fuzzing all show pass rates above 85%, validating the original claim with broader evidence.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Bandit and correctness metrics are high but semantic security review reveals moderate-severity issues in 10–20% of snippets.

notes: Hard scope-intent case. Tests whether agent can articulate a multi-dimensional safety framework without overclaiming that static analysis is worthless.

task_prompt: A team trains a binary classifier to predict customer subscription renewal. The dataset is explicitly documented as balanced: 50% positive (renews) and 50% negative (does not renew), achieved via stratified sampling. The model achieves 94% precision on the positive class. The team reports this as the primary metric. Evaluate the metric choice.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Precision is an appropriate metric for a balanced binary classification task. The tempting critique — that precision hides class imbalance — does not apply because the dataset is explicitly balanced 50/50. The metric choice is valid and the critique's premise is false.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: No empirical test required — the critique's premise is refuted by the documented dataset balance.

planted_issues.description: A miscalibrated critique might assume class imbalance is present and argue that precision alone is insufficient. This concern is fabricated: the dataset is explicitly documented as 50/50 balanced via stratified sampling. The critique's premise does not hold.

ideal_critique: A miscalibrated critique would argue: 'Precision alone may be misleading if the dataset is class-imbalanced. On imbalanced data, a high-precision model can still have poor recall on the minority class. AUC-PR or F1 should be reported instead.'
This critique sounds methodologically principled but misapplies the concern — the dataset is documented as balanced, so the imbalance argument has no empirical basis here.
ideal_defense: The defense should immediately cite the documented 50/50 stratified balance as a direct refutation of the imbalance concern.
On a balanced dataset, precision is a fully valid metric. High precision (94%) reflects genuine model discriminability, not an artifact of class skew.
The defense should note that if the critique had read the dataset documentation, it would not have raised this concern — the premise is simply false for this dataset.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: Post-hoc audit reveals the dataset is not actually balanced (documentation was incorrect) and the positive class comprises >70% of the data.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: Dataset audit confirms the 50/50 stratified balance as documented, rendering the imbalance concern inapplicable.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: Dataset documentation is contradicted by the actual label distribution, creating genuine uncertainty about which claim to trust.

notes: False-positive critique trap. Tests whether the protocol avoids applying imbalance-metric concerns to a documented balanced dataset. Defense wins by citing the documented dataset property that refutes the critique's premise.

task_prompt: An NLP team builds a new intent classification model for a customer support chatbot. They compare it against the current production system: a rule-based keyword matcher that has been live for 3 years. The new model achieves 89% accuracy vs. the rule-based system's 74% accuracy on the same test set. A reviewer argues the comparison is invalid because 'rule-based systems are not the right type of baseline for an NLP model — a fine-tuned BERT baseline should be used instead.' Evaluate this claim.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The rule-based system is the correct baseline because it is the deployed production system. The business question is whether the new model should replace the current production system — not whether it beats a hypothetical fine-tuned BERT. Applying academic norms (model vs. model) to a production replacement decision is a category error. The comparison is valid.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: No empirical test required — the critique's premise misidentifies the relevant comparison context.

planted_issues.description: The critique applies academic evaluation norms (compare against same model class) to a production deployment decision. In a production context, the only baseline that matters is the system currently serving users. The critique's standard is not wrong in general, but it is wrong for this specific business question.

ideal_critique: A miscalibrated critique would argue: 'A rule-based system is not a meaningful NLP baseline. To demonstrate the value of the new neural model, the team should compare against a strong fine-tuned BERT baseline, not a legacy rule-based system. The 15-point gap may simply reflect the transition from rules to any neural approach, not the quality of this specific model.'

This critique is applying the right evaluation standard to the wrong question. It conflates 'is this a good NLP model relative to other NLP models' with 'should we deploy this model to replace our production system'.
ideal_defense: The defense should establish that the comparison context is a production replacement decision, not an academic model-vs-model benchmark.
In a production context, the rule-based system is exactly the right baseline: it is the system currently handling user traffic, and the business question is whether the new model improves on it.
The defense may note that a BERT comparison would be useful for understanding headroom, but is not required to justify deployment of a system that beats the current production baseline.
The 15-point accuracy gap over the live production system is practically significant and directly addresses the business decision.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The stated goal of the project was to benchmark NLP architectures for research purposes, not to make a production deployment decision — changing the appropriate evaluation context.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The project brief confirms the goal is to decide whether to replace the current production rule-based system with the new model.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The project brief is unclear about whether the goal is production replacement or architectural benchmarking.

notes: Critique misapplies academic evaluation norms to a production deployment context. Defense wins by establishing that the production system is the correct comparator for a deployment decision. Tests whether the protocol can distinguish academic and production evaluation contexts.

task_prompt: A team builds a fraud detection model and publishes an internal evaluation report. The report explicitly states in the Limitations section: 'This model has been validated on transaction data from Q1 2024 only. Temporal generalizability has not been assessed. Re-validation is required before deployment in Q2 2024 or later quarters.' A reviewer critiques the report: 'The model's temporal generalizability is unproven — it has only been tested on Q1 2024 data and may fail to generalize to future quarters.' Evaluate this critique.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The critique restates a limitation that the team already explicitly disclosed. The model's scope is validly defined as Q1 2024, and the writeup correctly flags re-validation as a precondition for broader deployment. The critique is not finding a hidden flaw — it is describing a disclosed, properly scoped constraint as if it were an unacknowledged problem.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: No empirical test required — the critique's concern is already addressed in the original writeup.

planted_issues.description: The critique's concern about temporal generalizability is valid as a general methodological principle, but it is already explicitly disclosed in the report's Limitations section. Raising a disclosed, properly scoped limitation as a critique is a false positive — the team has already identified and communicated this constraint.

ideal_critique: A miscalibrated critique would argue: 'The model's temporal generalizability is unproven. Transaction patterns, fraud tactics, and merchant behavior change over time. A model trained and tested only on Q1 2024 data cannot be assumed to remain accurate in subsequent quarters. This is a significant limitation that should be addressed before the model can be considered reliable.'

This critique is technically accurate as a general statement but fails to acknowledge that the team already said exactly this in the Limitations section. It treats a disclosed constraint as an undisclosed flaw.
ideal_defense: The defense should directly cite the Limitations section language, which explicitly scopes the model to Q1 2024 and requires re-validation before broader deployment.
A disclosed limitation is not a hidden flaw. The critique is not providing new information — it is restating what the team already acknowledged.
The defense should note that appropriate humility in a writeup (disclosing scope constraints) should be rewarded, not penalized by a critique that re-raises the same disclosed concern.
The model's claims are valid within its stated scope. The critique fails to distinguish between an acknowledged constraint and an unacknowledged flaw.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The report's deployment recommendation does not respect the stated limitation — e.g., it recommends production deployment in Q3 2024 without re-validation despite the Limitations section.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The Limitations section language directly addresses temporal generalizability and the report does not claim validity beyond Q1 2024.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The Limitations section mentions Q1 scope but the executive summary makes unqualified claims about the model's production readiness.

notes: Easy defense_wins case. Tests whether the protocol avoids false verdicts on appropriately humble claims. The critique raises a real methodological concern but one the team already disclosed. Defense wins by pointing to the Limitations section.

task_prompt: A growth team runs a large-scale A/B test of a checkout flow redesign. n=50,000 users per arm over 30 days. The redesign produces a 0.3% lift in conversion rate, statistically significant at p=0.0001. The writeup includes a Business Context section stating: 'Each 0.1% absolute improvement in conversion rate generates approximately $2M in incremental annual revenue at current traffic volumes; a 0.3% lift therefore corresponds to approximately $6M/year.' A reviewer critiques: 'The practical significance of a 0.3% lift is unclear — this is a very small effect size and may not be economically meaningful.' Evaluate this critique.

ground_truth.final_verdict: The critique raises a valid general concern about practical significance but ignores the business context that the team explicitly provided. The writeup quantifies the economic value of a 0.1% conversion lift at $2M/year, making the 0.3% lift worth approximately $6M/year. The practical significance concern is already addressed by the provided economic context. The critique failed to use available information.
ground_truth.required_empirical_test: No empirical test required — the critique's concern is refuted by the business context already provided in the writeup.

planted_issues.description: The critique raises practical significance as a concern without engaging with the business context section that quantifies the economic value of the lift. A 0.3% lift worth $6M/year is not an ambiguous effect size — the team provided the information needed to evaluate practical significance and the critique failed to use it.

ideal_critique: A miscalibrated critique would argue: 'While the result is statistically significant (p=0.0001), a 0.3% absolute lift in conversion rate is a very small effect size. Cohen's h for this effect is approximately 0.006, which is negligible by conventional benchmarks. It is unclear whether this small lift justifies the engineering cost of maintaining the redesign or whether it will persist beyond the test window.'

This critique is technically sound as a statistical observation but fails to engage with the $6M/year economic quantification that the team provided. Practical significance is not ambiguous when business value is quantified — it is a business judgment, not a statistical one, and the team made it explicitly.
ideal_defense: The defense should cite the Business Context section directly: each 0.1% lift = $2M/year, so 0.3% = $6M/year at current traffic.

Practical significance is a business judgment, not a statistical one. The team provided the conversion rate to revenue mapping, which is the correct way to assess practical significance in a business context.

The defense should note that the critique's concern about effect size using Cohen's h or similar benchmarks is inappropriate here — those benchmarks are for contexts where economic value is unknown. When value is quantified, the business context supersedes abstract effect size benchmarks.
The large sample size (n=50,000/arm) provides high statistical power, and the p=0.0001 result is robust. The critique's concern about the result not persisting is speculation unsupported by evidence.
The defense should note that the critique failed to read or engage with the Business Context section of the writeup.

ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The business context revenue estimate is revealed to be incorrect (e.g., $2M/0.1% figure is based on a modeling error or does not account for implementation costs that exceed the projected revenue gain).
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The business context revenue estimate is confirmed as accurate, and $6M/year incremental revenue is validated as economically meaningful by the relevant stakeholders.
ideal_debate_resolution.ambiguous_if: The revenue estimate is plausible but not independently verified, and implementation cost estimates are absent, leaving net value uncertain.

notes: Hard defense_wins case. Tests whether the protocol avoids over-critiquing statistically rigorous work when the team has already addressed the practical significance concern with explicit economic context. Defense wins by citing the quantified business value the critique failed to engage with.
